name: Go
description: A simple, fast, statically typed language from Google. Excellent for building web servers, CLIs, and microservices.

lessons.title: Hello World & Packages
lessons.content: Every Go file belongs to a package. The 'main' package is the entry point. Import statements bring in libraries. fmt.Println prints output. Go uses := for short variable declaration.
lessons.code_example:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
    fmt.Println(42)
    fmt.Printf("Pi is approximately %.2f\n", 3.14159)
}

exercises.question: Which package must a Go program's entry point belong to?
hints: package main — this tells Go where execution begins

exercises.question: Which function prints output in Go?
hints: It belongs to the fmt (format) package

lessons.title: Variables & Types
lessons.content: Go has static typing. Use 'var' for explicit declaration or ':=' for short declaration (type inferred). Basic types: int, float64, string, bool. Multiple return values are a key Go feature.

exercises.question: What operator is used for short variable declaration in Go?
hints: It declares AND assigns in one step

lessons.title: Goroutines & Concurrency
lessons.content: Goroutines are lightweight threads managed by Go. Launch one with the 'go' keyword. Channels (chan) allow goroutines to communicate. This is Go's main superpower for writing concurrent programs.

exercises.question: What keyword launches a goroutine in Go?
hints: go sayHello() — the keyword is literally 'go'

lessons.title: Interfaces
lessons.content: Go interfaces define behaviour — a set of method signatures. Any type that implements all methods of an interface automatically satisfies it (implicit implementation). This makes Go very flexible.

exercises.question: How does a type implement an interface in Go?
options: Automatically, by implementing all its methods
exercises.answer: Automatically, by implementing all its methods
hints: Go uses implicit implementation — no 'implements' keyword needed
